content:
{
  "title": "Adversarially Aware Prompt Framing for LLM Robustness Against Jailbreaks",
  "problem": "Large language models are vulnerable to adversarial prompts and jailbreak attempts that bypass safety filters. Existing prompting methods often fail to anticipate or resist such attacks.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly deployed in public-facing applications, robust defenses against prompt-based attacks are crucial to prevent harmful outputs. Prior work has focused on model-side defenses rather than dynamic prompting strategies, leaving a gap for lightweight, easily integrated solutions.",
  "method": "We propose 'adversarially aware prompt framing,' where every user prompt is prefixed with a randomized, adversarially generated paraphrase that explicitly warns the model to reject manipulative or policy-violating requests. The paraphrase is sampled from a pool generated by an auxiliary LLM trained to simulate common jailbreak attempts. This dynamic prefixing seeks to inoculate the model against adversarial context by priming it to anticipate attacks. We hypothesize this will reduce the model's susceptibility to prompt injections and policy circumvention.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate this method using jailbreak prompt datasets (e.g., AdvBench, JailbreakBench) and compare against standard safety prompting and static safety preambles. Metrics will include attack success rate, harmlessness (as judged by automated classifiers), and language model utility on benign prompts (to assess any degradation in helpfulness or coherence)."
}
